philosophers.label: Alexander Bogdanov
key_works: A Short Course of Economic Science
philosophers.profile: Alexander Bogdanov develops a radically monistic and anti-substantialist philosophy in which experience is the primary material of both nature and consciousness. In empiriomonism, the opposition between the physical and the psychical is not ontological but organizational: both are complexes of experience ordered according to different practical and social standpoints. He rejects metaphysical dualism as well as any appeal to unknowable things-in-themselves, arguing that cognition is a collective labor of organizing experience for the purposes of orientation, prediction, and control. Truth is th
philosophers.stance: Bogdanov holds that reality is not divided into two fundamentally different realms of matter and mind; instead, both are organized complexes within a single experiential field. He argues that what we call the physical and the mental are alternative modes of ordering experience rather than distinct substances. He rejects the view that knowledge rests on access to things as they are independently of

philosophers.label: Gustavo Gutiérrez
key_works: The Power of the Poor in History
We Drink from Our Own Wells
philosophers.profile: Gustavo Gutiérrez articulates a theological-philosophical vision in which reality is grasped from the standpoint of historical praxis and the suffering of the poor. His ontology is neither a static metaphysics of essences nor a merely secular historicism; being is disclosed within a history marked by sin, domination, and the divine promise of liberation. Social structures are not accidental aggregates but historically sedimented forms of injustice, so the world must be interpreted as a conflictual totality in which grace and oppression intersect. Epistemologically, he advances a praxis-centere
philosophers.stance: Gutiérrez holds that reality is historically structured and morally charged: human beings inhabit a world shaped by material deprivation, domination, and communal struggle, yet open to transcendence and redemption. He argues that social reality is not neutral but organized by relations of power, and that any adequate account of the real must include both structural injustice and the possibility of

philosophers.label: Jeremy Rifkin
philosophers.profile: Jeremy Rifkin advances a civilizational critique of industrial capitalism centered on the transformation of energy regimes, communication infrastructures, and the social ontology generated by their convergence. His basic thesis is that modes of production are inseparable from thermodynamic conditions: every economic order is anchored in a specific energy matrix and in the institutional forms required to organize it. Ontologically, social reality is relational and infrastructural rather than atomistic; persons, markets, and institutions are constituted within networks of technological interdepe
philosophers.stance: Rifkin holds that reality is not best understood as a collection of isolated agents pursuing utility, but as an interdependent socio-ecological system structured by energy flows, technological networks, and biospheric constraints. He argues that the basic units of analysis are relations, infrastructures, and ecological limits rather than self-sufficient individuals or purely autonomous markets. Ab

philosophers.label: Julian of Norwich
philosophers.profile: Julian of Norwich articulates a speculative theology centered on divine love as the ontological ground, formal cause, and final end of all that is. Reality is understood as wholly sustained in being by God, not merely created at an initial moment but continuously held, enclosed, and preserved within divine charity. Her metaphysics is thus strongly participatory: creatures possess no autonomous self-subsistence, yet are not nullified, since their truth lies in their dependence upon God. Sin is treated not as a positive substance but as privation, lack, and occasion for humility, compassion, and
philosophers.stance: Julian of Norwich holds that reality is fundamentally grounded in a personal and all-sustaining divine source whose essence is love. She argues that created things are real but radically dependent, possessing their being through participation rather than self-subsistence. She rejects the view that evil has independent ontological status; sin is treated as deficiency and disorder rather than a coeq

philosophers.label: Adam Ferguson
key_works: An Essay on the History of Civil Society
Principles of Moral and Political Science
The History of the Progress and Termination of the Roman Republic
philosophers.profile: Adam Ferguson develops a social and moral philosophy centered on the irreducibly collective character of human life. Against atomistic accounts of persons as pre-political individuals, he treats sociability as constitutive of human nature: the species realizes its powers only in association, conflict, cooperation, and civic participation. His ontology is thus practical and historical rather than metaphysically system-building; human reality is disclosed in institutions, customs, and the emergent order of civil society. He is especially attentive to unintended consequences: social forms arise f
philosophers.stance: Ferguson holds that reality, at least in the human domain, is fundamentally social and historical rather than adequately understood as a mere aggregate of isolated individuals. He argues that institutions, norms, and political orders are real formations emerging from human practices, often through unintended consequences. He rejects the view that social order is simply the product of explicit cont

philosophers.label: Lawrence Kohlberg
key_works: Stage and Sequence: The Cognitive-Developmental Approach to Socialization
The Meaning and Measurement of Moral Development
philosophers.profile: Lawrence Kohlberg develops a cognitivist and developmental account of practical reason in which moral judgment exhibits an invariant sequence of structural transformations. His central thesis is that the form of moral reasoning, rather than the substantive content of particular norms, discloses the level of moral development. Ontologically, he does not construct a heavy metaphysics of substances or essences; instead, the moral domain is treated as a real and rational order of claims concerning justice, reciprocity, rights, and the universalizability of norms. Moral reality is thus neither redu
philosophers.stance: Kohlberg holds that reality in the moral domain includes objective problems of justice that cannot be reduced to custom, authority, or subjective desire. He argues that moral distinctions are not mere projections of feeling, though access to them depends on the development of cognitive structures through social interaction. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds that moral knowledge is construct

philosophers.label: Judah Loew ben Bezalel
philosophers.profile: Judah Loew ben Bezalel articulates a metaphysics in which created being is ordered through graded forms, symbolic correspondences, and teleological structure grounded in divine wisdom. Reality is neither reducible to bare materiality nor exhausted by discursive abstraction; it is a hierarchically articulated cosmos in which the sensible, juridical, and spiritual dimensions interpenetrate. His ontology emphasizes the distinction and coordination of form and matter, actuality and potentiality, nature and what exceeds nature, while insisting that Torah discloses the inner ratio of creation rather
philosophers.stance: He holds that reality is an intelligible, purposive order grounded in a transcendent divine source, with different levels of being linked by formal and symbolic relations. He argues that the world is not a self-sufficient aggregate of facts but a structured whole whose deepest meaning is disclosed through revelation and disciplined interpretation. On knowledge, he holds that reason is genuine but

philosophers.label: Alain de Benoist
philosophers.profile: Alain de Benoist develops a metapolitical critique of liberal modernity centered on the dissolution of collective forms of life by individualism, economism, and universalist moral abstraction. His ontology is pluralist and differentialist: human beings are not atomized bearers of interchangeable rights but historically situated, embodied, and culturally mediated beings whose identities arise within concrete communities, symbolic orders, and inherited lifeworlds. He therefore opposes both reductive materialism and homogenizing universalism, defending a view of reality as irreducibly plural, con
philosophers.stance: He holds that reality is not best understood as a collection of self-sufficient individuals but as a plurality of historically formed communities, symbolic worlds, and irreducible differences. He argues that human beings are socially and culturally constituted, and rejects the view that abstract universal categories can exhaust political or moral reality. philosophers.label: Giovanni Papini
philosophers.profile: Giovanni Papini develops a radically agonistic and voluntarist conception of thought in which philosophy is less the contemplative adequation of intellect to being than an existential and polemical act of self-assertion. His ontology is unstable, anti-systematic, and marked by a recurrent tension between nihilating critique and the search for absolute meaning: reality appears at once as flux, conflict, and insufficiency, yet also as ordered toward transcendence and conversion. He resists any closed metaphysic of substance or purely positivist account of the real, treating the world as spiritua
philosophers.stance: Papini holds that reality is not exhausted by material facts or by a stable rational order transparently available to detached inquiry; he argues that the real is dynamic, spiritually significant, and disclosed most fully in existential crisis and conversion. He rejects the view that knowledge rests solely on impersonal demonstration or sensory accumulation, holding instead that belief, action, te

philosophers.label: Ananda Coomaraswamy
key_works: Christian and Oriental Philosophy of Art
philosophers.profile: Ananda Coomaraswamy develops a rigorously traditionalist metaphysics centered on the distinction between the Absolute and its contingent manifestations. Reality is hierarchically ordered: the sensible world is not self-sufficient but a domain of symbols, vestiges, and formal expressions participating in principial being. He treats myth, ritual, and sacred art as modes of intellection rather than mere cultural productions; their truth lies in their anagogical function, leading the knower from appearance to archetype. His ontology is thus neither reductively material nor merely psychological, bu
philosophers.stance: Coomaraswamy holds that reality is fundamentally hierarchical and symbolic: ultimate reality is absolute, unconditioned, and more real than the empirical world, while finite things possess intelligibility through participation in transcendent principles. He argues that knowledge is grounded not primarily in sensation or autonomous reasoning but in intellectual intuition, contemplative insight, and

philosophers.label: Giovanni Battista Riccioli
key_works: Geographiae et Hydrographiae Reformatae Libri Duodecim
philosophers.profile: Giovanni Battista Riccioli articulates a learned scholastic natural philosophy in which cosmology, physics, and theology are ordered within a hierarchical ontology of created substances governed by divine wisdom. His thought presupposes a realist metaphysics: celestial and terrestrial bodies possess determinate natures, causal powers, and teleological order, and the world is intelligible because it is the product of a rational creator. In epistemology, he combines Aristotelian demonstratio with mathematically informed observation, insisting that astronomical hypotheses must be weighed not only
philosophers.stance: Riccioli holds that reality is a mind-independent, ordered cosmos composed of substances with real natures and causal powers. He argues that the structure of the world cannot be settled by mathematical elegance alone; a true account of reality must also satisfy physical explanation and metaphysical coherence. He rejects the view that predictive success by itself establishes ontology. About the gro

philosophers.label: Thomas Reid
key_works: An Inquiry into the Human Mind on the Principles of Common Sense
Essays on the Intellectual Powers of Man
Essays on the Active Powers of Man
concepts: rejection of the theory of ideas
philosophers.profile: Thomas Reid develops a philosophy of common sense in explicit opposition to the "theory of ideas" and the skeptical consequences he takes to follow from representationalism. In ontology, he is a robust realist: external objects, other minds, and the enduring self exist independently of our apprehension of them, and perception is not primarily awareness of internal images but a direct, non-inferential relation to mind-independent things. His account of mind is faculty-based, distinguishing sensation from perception, and emphasizing active powers, liberty, and the irreducibility of agency. Epist
philosophers.stance: Reid holds that reality consists of a mind-independent world, genuinely existing persons, and real causal powers; he rejects the view that what we immediately know are only internal representations from which the external world must be inferred. He argues that the grounds of knowledge lie in the proper functioning of our natural faculties and in first principles that are rationally basic though no

philosophers.label: Alexandros of Aphrodisias
philosophers.profile: Alexandros of Aphrodisias articulates a rigorously Peripatetic metaphysics centered on substance, form, matter, causality, and the explanatory primacy of actuality over potentiality. Against Platonizing separations, he treats universals as posterior to and abstracted from particulars rather than as independently subsisting entities. His ontology is thus a robust hylomorphism: concrete beings are composites whose intelligibility depends on formal and final causes, while motion and change require the actuality of an unmoved divine intellect as ultimate cause. In psychology and epistemology, he d
philosophers.stance: Alexandros holds that reality is fundamentally composed of concrete substances structured by form and matter, and that intelligibility lies in causal and teleological organization rather than in separately existing abstract entities. He argues that universals do not exist independently of particulars, and rejects the view that fate or necessity exhausts the explanation of action. About the grounds

philosophers.label: Étienne Gilson
key_works: The Christian Philosophy of St. Thomas Aquinas
History of Christian Philosophy in the Middle Ages
philosophers.profile: Étienne Gilson develops a distinctly metaphysical Thomism centered on the primacy of esse, the act of being, over essence and conceptual abstraction. Against essentialist reductions of metaphysics to logic or epistemology, he argues that being is first encountered in existential judgment and that the proper object of metaphysics is ens inquantum ens as grounded in the actus essendi. Created beings are composites of essence and existence; only in God are essence and esse identical, making divine ipsum esse subsistens the ultimate explanatory principle of finite reality. His ontology is therefor
philosophers.stance: Gilson holds that reality is fundamentally composed of beings whose intelligibility depends on an act of existence more primary than their definable essence. He argues that finite things are real independently of thought and are ordered by participation toward a transcendent source in which existence is not received but identical with what it is. He rejects the view that philosophy begins from sel

philosophers.label: Edward de Bono
key_works: Lateral Thinking: Creativity Step by Step
I Am Right, You Are Wrong
philosophers.profile: Edward de Bono develops a pragmatics of thinking centered on the claim that cognition is not exhausted by truth-functional analysis, deduction, or adversarial critique. His core thesis is that mind operates as a self-organizing patterning system: perception and thought stabilize into recurrent tracks, and creativity consists in the deliberate restructuring of these patterns. Ontologically, he is not a systematic metaphysician, but he treats reality as encountered through pattern-sensitive perceptual organization rather than through transparent access by pure logic alone. Epistemologically, he
philosophers.stance: De Bono holds that reality is not best approached solely through linear analysis, because human access to it is mediated by pattern-forming perception. He argues that thought tends to become trapped in established cognitive tracks, so novelty requires deliberate techniques for restructuring how situations are seen. He rejects the view that knowledge is grounded only in deduction, proof, or adversa

philosophers.label: Ronald Dworkin
philosophers.profile: Ronald Dworkin develops a normative jurisprudence in which law is not exhausted by pedigree-based rules but includes principles with dimension of weight, embedded in the best moral interpretation of institutional practice. Against semantic and conventionalist accounts, he argues that legal propositions are truth-apt because they are made true by the rights and responsibilities that follow from constructive interpretation: the interpreter must fit the legal materials while justifying them under the value of political integrity. Ontologically, his mature view is neither reductive naturalism nor
philosophers.stance: Dworkin holds that reality includes objective normative truths: legal and moral facts are not reducible to physical facts, social conventions, or subjective attitudes. He argues that the content of law depends not only on enacted sources but on the morally best interpretation of a community's institutional history. About the grounds of knowledge, he rejects the view that moral or legal judgment is

philosophers.label: Władysław Tarnowski
philosophers.profile: Władysław Tarnowski is not principally recognized as a philosopher, and no established philosophical corpus or technical doctrine is attributed to him in the standard history of philosophy. On the basis of the available description, he belongs primarily to the domain of musical composition rather than to a determinate metaphysical, epistemological, or ethical school. Accordingly, no rigorous reconstruction of an ontology, theory of cognition, or normative ethics in the strict sense can be responsibly ascribed to him. At most, one may infer a broadly aesthetic orientation in which artistic form
philosophers.stance: No determinate philosophical stance can be securely attributed to Tarnowski on the basis of the available evidence. He is not known to have produced philosophical texts or to have defended explicit positions about the nature of reality, the grounds of knowledge, or the basis of ethics. One therefore cannot responsibly say that he holds that reality is fundamentally material, mental, dual, or other

philosophers.label: Mathurin Jacques Brisson
philosophers.profile: Mathurin Jacques Brisson’s philosophical profile is best understood through natural philosophy ordered by classificatory reason. His ontology is neither speculative metaphysics nor reductionist mechanism in a strict sense, but a realism about natural kinds as disclosed through the stable organization of bodies. He treats nature as articulated into determinate structures whose differences are not merely nominal but grounded in observable form, proportion, and function. The individual organism is thus a legitimate unit of inquiry, and the taxonomic order of species and genera expresses an intell
philosophers.stance: Brisson holds that reality is composed of mind-independent natural entities whose properties and relations can be discovered through careful examination. He argues that nature exhibits real order and that classification is not simply a linguistic imposition but tracks objective similarities and differences. He rejects the view that knowledge of nature can be secured primarily by speculative deduct

philosophers.label: Robert Recorde
philosophers.profile: Robert Recorde does not present a fully systematized philosophical doctrine in the manner of a metaphysician, yet his writings embody a distinctive epistemic and ontological orientation grounded in mathematical intelligibility. Reality is treated as structured, measurable, and susceptible to exact symbolic articulation. Number, proportion, and demonstrative order function not merely as computational conveniences but as privileged media through which the rational form of things becomes accessible. His use of notation, most famously the sign of equality, expresses a commitment to identity, equiv
philosophers.stance: Recorde holds that reality is, at least in significant domains, ordered in ways that can be represented through quantitative relations and formal symbols. He argues that knowledge is best grounded not in inherited authority alone but in demonstrable procedure, intelligible notation, and reproducible reasoning. He rejects the view that learning should remain dependent on opaque technical language a

philosophers.label: Q77057
philosophers.profile: Friedrich Nietzsche develops a radical critique of metaphysics, morality, and truth by treating them as historically generated interpretations rather than disclosures of a mind-independent order endowed with fixed essences or purposes. His ontology is anti-substantialist and dynamic: the world is best understood through the concept of will to power, not as a single psychological drive but as a plural agon of forces, each striving to expand, interpret, and organize. Becoming is primary; stable being is a grammatical and practical fiction imposed upon flux. Epistemologically, he rejects the idea
philosophers.stance: Nietzsche holds that reality is not composed of fixed substances governed by intrinsic purposes, but is a field of becoming structured by competing forces and interpretations. He argues that what is often called truth is an achievement of perspective rather than a mirror of reality, and rejects the view that knowledge can be grounded in a detached, universal standpoint. philosophers.label: Richard von Krafft-Ebing
key_works: Arbeiten aus dem Gesamtgebiet der Psychiatrie und Neuropathologie
philosophers.profile: Richard von Krafft-Ebing develops a medico-psychiatric taxonomy of sexual life in which erotic desire is treated as a domain of psychopathology, forensic relevance, and biological function. His basic ontological assumption is naturalistic: the psyche is continuous with the nervous organism, and disturbances of instinct, affect, and volition are to be understood as deviations within a psychophysical economy rather than as purely moral failings. Sexuality is construed teleologically in relation to reproduction, so non-procreative forms of desire are classified under perversion, inversion, fetish
philosophers.stance: Krafft-Ebing holds that reality, at least in the human domain, is fundamentally psychophysical: mental life is rooted in bodily and neurological processes, and disorders of desire are real natural phenomena rather than merely moral or theological categories. He argues that knowledge of such phenomena is grounded in clinical evidence, comparative case analysis, and classificatory inference, not in

philosophers.label: Criss Angel
philosophers.profile: Criss Angel is not recognized as a philosopher in the disciplinary sense, and no established corpus of philosophical doctrine, technical treatise, or systematic position is attributed to him in the history of philosophy. The conceptual material associated with his public work belongs instead to performance, illusion, spectacle, and the manipulation of perceptual expectation. If one were to reconstruct an implicit philosophical profile from that practice, it would center on the instability of immediate appearance, the gap between phenomenal presentation and causal reality, and the ease with whi
philosophers.stance: Criss Angel does not appear to hold or defend a documented philosophical system. He has not, in the relevant scholarly sense, argued a worked-out position about the nature of reality, the grounds of knowledge, or the basis of ethics. At most, his performances can be read as illustrating that appearances are often unreliable, that observers readily infer hidden powers from incomplete evidence, and

philosophers.label: Jean-François Marmontel
key_works: Les Incas, ou la destruction de l'empire du Pérou
Mémoires d'un père pour servir à l'instruction de ses enfants
philosophers.profile: Jean-François Marmontel belongs to the broad Enlightenment project of philosophic critique, moral pedagogy, and civil refinement. His thought is less a systematic metaphysics than a practical philosophy articulated through essays, tales, historiography, and literary theory. Ontologically, he presupposes a largely secular and intelligible order of nature governed by regularity rather than miracle, and a human world structured by passions, interests, sociability, and institutions. He treats human beings as naturally sentient and affective agents whose moral and political formation depends on edu
philosophers.stance: Marmontel holds that reality is a lawful natural and social order accessible to human inquiry without appeal to obscurantist explanations. He argues that human beings are fundamentally affective, social, and educable, and rejects the view that moral life depends primarily on revelation or unquestioned tradition. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds that justified belief arises from experience,

philosophers.label: Epicharmus of Kos
philosophers.profile: Epicharmus is associated with an early form of philosophical reflection in which comic, gnomic, and quasi-Pythagorean elements converge around questions of identity, measure, and intelligence. The surviving fragments suggest an ontology attentive to flux and numerical or formal order: things in the sensible domain are unstable, liable to alteration from moment to moment, so that ordinary predication about enduring sameness becomes problematic. Against naïve assumptions of fixed identity, he emphasizes processes of becoming and the difficulty of saying that what has changed remains simply the s
philosophers.stance: Epicharmus holds that reality, as encountered in ordinary experience, is marked by change rather than simple permanence. He argues that common assumptions about stable identity are unreliable, since things undergoing alteration cannot be straightforwardly treated as numerically and qualitatively the same in every respect. At the same time, he rejects the view that reality is therefore unintelligib

philosophers.label: Ban Zhao
key_works: Continuation and redaction of the Hanshu
philosophers.profile: Ban Zhao articulates a learned Confucian vision in which cosmic, social, and moral order form a continuous normative pattern. Her ontology is not speculative in a metaphysical sense, but assumes a world structured by Heaven, ritual differentiation, and relational roles, where harmony depends on the proper calibration of conduct within the family and polity. Human beings are constituted through patterned relations rather than isolated individuality; the self is cultivated by disciplined participation in li, deference, and moral learning. Epistemically, she treats knowledge as inseparable from s
philosophers.stance: Ban Zhao holds that reality is fundamentally normative and relational: the world of human life is structured by ordered roles, patterned distinctions, and a larger moral-cosmic harmony rather than by atomized individuals pursuing self-chosen ends. She argues that knowledge is grounded in disciplined learning, interpretation of authoritative texts, historical understanding, and reflective self-cult

philosophers.label: Edward Abbey
concepts: intrinsic value of the nonhuman world
philosophers.profile: Edward Abbey advances a militant environmental humanism centered on the intrinsic value of wild nature and the corrupting force of technocratic civilization. His ontology is neither systematic metaphysics nor naturalistic reductionism in a strict academic sense; rather, it treats wilderness as a reality with its own integrity, anterior to and resistant against instrumental appropriation. He opposes the conversion of land into resource, scenery, or administratively managed object, and thus rejects an anthropocentric ontology in which beings derive significance solely from human use. Epistemical
philosophers.stance: Abbey holds that reality is not exhausted by human projects, economic functions, or administrative categories; the nonhuman world possesses a value and integrity independent of its usefulness to us. He argues that genuine knowledge is grounded less in detached system-building than in firsthand encounter, perceptual intimacy, and lived experience of place. philosophers.label: Gregory of Narek
key_works: Commentary on the Song of Songs
History of the Holy Cross of Aparank
philosophers.profile: Gregory of Narek articulates a profoundly theocentric and penitential metaphysics in which all being is grounded in the inexhaustible plenitude of God, while the creature exists in ontological dependence, fragility, and moral woundedness. Reality is hierarchically ordered: the Creator alone possesses fullness of life and incorruptible goodness, whereas the human person, though bearing a divine orientation, experiences existence under the conditions of sin, mutability, and inner division. His epistemology is inseparable from ascetic self-knowledge. Genuine knowledge does not arise primarily fro
philosophers.stance: Gregory of Narek holds that reality is fundamentally grounded in a transcendent and personal divine source on which all finite beings depend. He argues that the deepest truth about the human condition is not self-sufficiency but estrangement, vulnerability, and the need for reconciliation. He rejects the view that reality can be adequately understood through autonomous reason alone; knowledge of w

philosophers.label: Michel Aflaq
philosophers.profile: Michel Aflaq articulates a doctrine in which the Arab nation is not merely an aggregate of populations but a living historical-spiritual totality possessing an immanent vocation. His ontology is thus neither atomistic nor narrowly materialist: the primary social reality is the nation as an organic unity whose essence unfolds through history in language, memory, and collective struggle. Individual existence receives its fullest significance through participation in this supra-individual mission. Epistemically, Aflaq privileges historical consciousness, political awakening, and the recovery of a
philosophers.stance: Aflaq holds that reality in the political-human domain is structured not primarily by isolated individuals but by historically constituted collective unities, above all the nation understood as a real bearer of meaning and agency. He argues that a people has an essence expressed in language, memory, and common destiny, and rejects the view that political order can be grounded solely in procedural

philosophers.label: Mazdak
philosophers.profile: Mazdak advances a radically dualist and reformist cosmology in which the world is structured by the mixture of two primordial principles, Light and Darkness. Light is characterized by cognition, purposiveness, and beneficence, whereas Darkness is marked by blindness, disorder, and harmful impulse. Human existence belongs to the tragic domain of admixture: conflict, domination, and possessiveness arise not from an original norm of being but from the contingent interpenetration of opposed principles. Ontologically, therefore, evil is not merely privative but has a quasi-substantial status within
philosophers.stance: Mazdak holds that reality is fundamentally divided between opposed principles of order and disorder, goodness and harmfulness, and that the experienced world is the unstable result of their mixture. He argues that evil is not simply ignorance or lack, but is rooted in a real antagonistic factor within the structure of the cosmos. philosophers.label: Chen Duxiu
key_works: The Way of Confucius and Modern Life
Letter to All Comrades of the Communist Party of China
philosophers.profile: Chen Duxiu’s philosophical outlook is best understood as a militant synthesis of anti-traditional iconoclasm, scientific naturalism, democratic individualism, and later historical materialism. At the ontological level, he rejects metaphysical transcendence and inherited cosmological hierarchies in favor of an immanent, this-worldly account of social reality structured by material conditions, institutional power, and historical conflict. Human life is not ordered by timeless ritual essences but by mutable social relations susceptible to critique and transformation. Epistemologically, he privile
philosophers.stance: Chen Duxiu holds that reality is fundamentally historical, social, and material rather than grounded in sacred order or immutable moral hierarchy. He argues that institutions, norms, and identities are products of human practices and power relations, and therefore can be remade. philosophers.label: Syed Abul Ala Maududi
key_works: Purdah and the Status of Woman in Islam
philosophers.profile: Syed Abul Ala Maududi develops a comprehensive Islamic philosophy of din as an all-encompassing normative order in which tawhid functions not merely as a theological proposition but as the ontological and political principle of reality. Since sovereignty belongs exclusively to God (hakimiyyat), all legitimate authority is derivative and fiduciary; the human being is khalifah, vicegerent rather than autonomous legislator. Ontologically, the cosmos is a purposive creation ordered by divine command, and human nature is structured by fitrah, which disposes it toward recognition of revelation and m
philosophers.stance: Maududi holds that reality is fundamentally theocentric: the world is a created, intelligible, morally ordered whole grounded in a transcendent divine will. He argues that human beings do not own ultimate sovereignty over themselves or their societies, but exercise delegated stewardship under a higher normative order. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds that reason and experience are genuine

philosophers.label: Celsus
philosophers.profile: Celsus advances a polemical but philosophically articulate defense of a hierarchically ordered cosmos governed by divine reason, civic piety, and inherited cultic forms. His ontology is broadly theistic and cosmological: reality is not a scene of arbitrary intervention but a rationally articulated whole in which the supreme god stands above a graded multiplicity of subordinate divine powers, daimones, peoples, and laws. He rejects the claim that a single community enjoys exclusive access to truth or providence, insisting instead on the distributed manifestation of the divine through the plural
philosophers.stance: Celsus holds that reality is an ordered, intelligible whole governed by a highest divine principle and articulated through subordinate powers and established forms of life. He argues that the world is not the product of a jealous or exclusive deity acting for one small group, and rejects the view that truth is confined to a single revelation or community. philosophers.label: Wolfgang Köhler
key_works: The Place of Value in a World of Facts
philosophers.profile: Wolfgang Köhler develops a non-atomistic account of mind and world in which structured wholes are ontologically and epistemically prior to aggregates of elements. Against associationism and psychophysical atomism, he argues that perceptual and cognitive life is organized by Gestalten: dynamic, internally articulated totalities whose properties are not reducible to the sum of local sensations. His ontology is thus field-theoretic and relational. Physical and psychological processes exhibit isomorphism, not as crude identity, but as a lawful correspondence between phenomenal organization and cer
philosophers.stance: Köhler holds that reality is fundamentally structured: wholes, patterns, and field-relations are not derivative conveniences but basic features of both nature and experience. He argues that the world cannot be adequately described as a mere aggregate of independent parts, and rejects the view that complex phenomena are fully explained by summing elementary units. philosophers.label: Ernst Zermelo
key_works: Untersuchungen über die Grundlagen der Mengenlehre I
Beweis, dass jede Menge wohlgeordnet werden kann
Neuer Beweis für die Möglichkeit einer Wohlordnung
philosophers.profile: Ernst Zermelo’s philosophical significance lies in the axiomatization of set theory and in a broadly realist, objectivist conception of mathematical existence. He treats mathematics not as a mere calculus of symbols but as a theory of determinate ideal domains whose internal articulation can be rendered through explicit axioms. Ontologically, his work presupposes the legitimacy of sets as ideal objects, while simultaneously restricting unrestricted comprehension in order to avoid contradiction. The resulting framework replaces naive totalization with a stratified conception of set-formation go
philosophers.stance: Zermelo holds that reality includes an abstract mathematical domain whose objects are not reducible to physical things, linguistic conventions, or mental constructions. He argues that mathematics concerns objectively valid structures and rejects the view that mathematical existence must be tied to explicit construction. philosophers.label: Sosigenes of Alexandria
philosophers.profile: Sosigenes of Alexandria is known primarily as an astronomer and mathematical expert rather than as a systematic philosopher, so no independent corpus permits a full reconstruction of a comprehensive doctrine in ontology, epistemology, and ethics. The philosophical profile attributable to him is therefore inferential and tied to the exact sciences. His intellectual orientation appears to presuppose a kosmos governed by stable, intelligible regularities susceptible to arithmetical and geometrical determination. Ontologically, this implies commitment to an ordered natural world whose celestial mo
philosophers.stance: Sosigenes holds that reality, at least in its celestial and temporal dimensions, is structured, regular, and objectively intelligible. He argues that the world is not fundamentally disclosed through inherited convention alone, but through the disciplined comparison of observed cycles and mathematical representation. About the nature of reality, he appears to assume a form of cosmological realism:

philosophers.label: Marcus Cornelius Fronto
philosophers.profile: Marcus Cornelius Fronto is best understood not as a systematic metaphysician but as a theorist of eloquentia whose implicit philosophy is organized around the formative power of language. His central commitment is that verbal expression is not a merely external vehicle for thought; diction, copia verborum, archaism, and stylistic discrimination shape judgment itself. Ontologically, he does not advance a developed doctrine of substance or cosmos, yet he presupposes a practical world of civic action in which human affairs are mediated by speech, memory, and exempla. Reality, for Fronto, is encou
philosophers.stance: Fronto holds that reality is not primarily disclosed through speculative metaphysics but through the concrete domain of human action, civic relations, and the practical situations in which speech operates. He argues that language is not a neutral container for antecedent thoughts; it partly constitutes the clarity, force, and discriminative precision of thought itself. On the grounds of knowledge,

philosophers.label: Hermann Lotze
key_works: Medizinische Psychologie oder Physiologie der Seele
philosophers.profile: Hermann Lotze develops a teleologically inflected ideal-realism that seeks to reconcile the mechanistic intelligibility of nature with the primacy of value, personality, and meaning. Ontologically, he rejects both crude materialism and an abstract absolute idealism: the world of interacting things is not illusory, yet its coherence depends upon an underlying spiritual unity in which relations, laws, and purposive order are grounded. Mechanism is for him a legitimate form of scientific explanation, but never self-sufficient metaphysics; causal nexus describes the mode of connection among phenom
philosophers.stance: Lotze holds that reality is neither mere matter in motion nor a self-unfolding system of pure concepts. He argues that the natural world is real and law-governed, but that its ultimate intelligibility depends on value, purposiveness, and a unifying spiritual ground. He rejects the view that mechanical explanation is sufficient as a final account of being, while also rejecting the view that empiric

philosophers.label: Rigas Velestinlis
key_works: New Political Constitution of the Inhabitants of Rumeli, Asia Minor, the Mediterranean Islands, and Wallachia
Declaration of the Rights of Man
philosophers.profile: Rigas Velestinlis articulates a political philosophy in which freedom is not merely a juridical status but the collective condition of a people capable of self-legislation. His central thesis is that legitimate order arises from popular sovereignty rather than dynastic title, inherited hierarchy, or sacralized domination. Ontologically, the political community is treated as a moral and civic body constituted by free and equal agents whose common life is not reducible to private interest; the polis is a normative association grounded in shared liberty. Epistemologically, he privileges public re
philosophers.stance: Velestinlis holds that political reality is not naturally organized by hereditary rank or transcendent sanction, but ought to be constituted through the equal standing of persons under laws they can regard as their own. He argues that the most important social facts are institutions of domination or freedom, and that tyranny is not a natural order but a contingent and unjust arrangement. About the

philosophers.label: Alfred Schütz
key_works: The Phenomenology of the Social World
Reflections on the Problem of Relevance
philosophers.profile: Alfred Schütz develops a phenomenological sociology centered on the structures of the Lebenswelt, the pregiven world of everyday experience within which actors orient themselves pragmatically before any theoretical thematization. His ontology is neither a metaphysics of substances nor a reduction to brute facts, but an analysis of the socially constituted world as meaningful reality: the world is encountered as already typified, sedimented, and organized through systems of relevance. Social reality is constituted intersubjectively through reciprocal perspectives, idealizations such as the inte
philosophers.stance: Schütz holds that reality, as relevant for human life, is not exhausted by physical objects but includes a socially constituted world of meanings, institutions, and typified expectations. He argues that this world is objectively experienced only because intersubjective practices stabilize it; thus he rejects the view that social reality is either merely subjective projection or fully independent o

philosophers.label: Ziya Gökalp
philosophers.profile: Ziya Gökalp develops a normative social philosophy in which the individual is intelligible only within the moral and symbolic totality of collective life. His ontology is neither atomistic nor merely materialist: society is a sui generis reality constituted by shared representations, institutions, language, custom, and ideal values. The social fact is not reducible to private psychology, since conscience itself is socially mediated through terbiye, mefkûre, and the internalization of communal norms. Epistemically, he treats sociology as the privileged science for grasping the objective structu
philosophers.stance: Gökalp holds that reality includes irreducibly social structures: norms, institutions, and collective meanings are real features of human life, not mere aggregates of individual preferences. He argues that persons become intelligible through participation in a shared moral order, and rejects the view that society can be explained adequately by psychological or economic individualism alone. About t

philosophers.label: Guarino Guarini
key_works: Euclides adauctus et methodicus mathematicaque universalis
philosophers.profile: Guarino Guarini does not present a systematic philosophy in the manner of a scholastic metaphysician, yet his thought can be reconstructed from the convergence of mathematical speculation, architectural theory, and theological cosmology. His ontology is fundamentally ordered, hierarchical, and intelligible: being is articulated through proportion, measure, and geometric relation, so that form is not merely decorative accident but a disclosure of rational structure within creation. Number and geometry function as mediating principles between sensible multiplicity and higher intelligible order.
philosophers.stance: Guarini holds that reality is intrinsically ordered and that its order is accessible through formal relations, especially mathematical and geometric ones. He argues that the sensible world is not self-sufficient chaos but an articulated domain whose intelligibility reflects a higher rational structure. He rejects the view that beauty is merely subjective ornament; instead, aesthetic form can discl

philosophers.label: D. T. Suzuki
philosophers.profile: D. T. Suzuki articulates a Zen-inflected Mahayana vision in which ultimate reality is not a substantial entity but the dynamic suchness of things apprehended prior to the bifurcation of subject and object. Drawing on notions such as sunyata, tathata, prajna, and satori, he presents being as nondual activity rather than as a collection of self-subsistent substances. The ordinary discriminative intellect operates through conceptual fixation and thereby reifies distinctions that are pragmatically useful but metaphysically derivative. Genuine knowing arises through direct, non-discursive awakening
philosophers.stance: Suzuki holds that reality is fundamentally nondual and cannot be adequately captured by substance metaphysics or by the opposition between mind and world. He argues that ordinary conceptual thought abstracts from a more primordial field of experience and therefore mistakes its own distinctions for ultimate structure. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds that discursive reasoning has instrument

philosophers.label: Voltairine de Cleyre
philosophers.profile: Voltairine de Cleyre develops an anti-authoritarian social philosophy grounded in the primacy of individual sovereignty, voluntary association, and the critique of institutionalized domination. Her ontology is not metaphysically system-building but socially diagnostic: the decisive realities are relations of power, coercive apparatuses, and the lived capacities of persons for self-direction, mutual aid, and free cooperation. The state, church, law, and capitalist property regimes appear not as neutral forms but as sedimented structures of compulsion that deform human flourishing. Epistemically
philosophers.stance: De Cleyre holds that social reality is fundamentally shaped by power relations rather than by legitimate hierarchies, and argues that institutions claiming authority over persons are typically forms of organized coercion. She rejects the view that order requires centralized command, maintaining instead that cooperative life can arise from voluntary association and reciprocal respect. On knowledge,

philosophers.label: Christian Fürchtegott Gellert
key_works: Briefe, nebst einer praktischen Abhandlung von dem guten Geschmacke in Briefen
Das Leben der schwedischen Gräfin von G***
philosophers.profile: Christian Fürchtegott Gellert articulates a moral philosophy centered less on systematic metaphysics than on the cultivation of virtue through sentiment, practical reason, and religiously inflected moral self-knowledge. His implicit ontology is theistic and providential: the world is ordered by a wise moral author, and human beings occupy it as finite, dependent, yet accountable agents. The structure of reality is therefore not morally indifferent; it is teleologically aligned with the distinction between virtue and vice. Epistemically, he privileges forms of knowledge accessible to common hum
philosophers.stance: Gellert holds that reality is fundamentally ordered by a divine moral intelligence, so that the world is not a value-neutral aggregate of facts but a setting for responsible agency. He argues that human beings are finite creatures whose proper understanding of themselves depends on recognizing dependence, accountability, and the moral significance of ordinary conduct. About knowledge, he holds tha

philosophers.label: Tom Regan
philosophers.profile: Tom Regan develops a rights-based moral theory whose central ontological and ethical claim is that many nonhuman animals are not mere resources but bearers of inherent value. Against aggregative and preference-satisfaction models, he argues that individuals who are “subjects-of-a-life” possess a kind of value that is not reducible to usefulness, social recognition, or contribution to overall welfare. The relevant ontology is therefore morally individualist: what fundamentally matters are experiencing subjects with beliefs, desires, perception, memory, a sense of the future, emotional life, and
philosophers.stance: Regan holds that reality includes a morally significant class of individuals whose inner lives make them ends in themselves. He argues that value is not exhausted by impersonal states of affairs or aggregate utility; rather, it inheres in subjects who have a welfare of their own. On knowledge, he holds that moral knowledge is grounded in rational ethical reflection, conceptual consistency, and the

philosophers.label: Pierre Duhem
key_works: The Aim and Structure of Physical Theory
philosophers.profile: Pierre Duhem develops a philosophy of science centered on the logical structure, autonomy, and limits of physical theory. He denies that theoretical physics yields a literal unveiling of hidden essences; its proper task is the economical and systematic representation of experimental laws by means of mathematically articulated propositions. Ontologically, he is cautious and anti-metaphysical at the level of physics itself: physical theory does not decide between rival pictures of ultimate reality, since its symbols underdetermine the real constitution of nature. Yet this is not crude instrument
philosophers.stance: Duhem holds that reality is not exhausted by the constructs of scientific theory: the world is real, but theoretical physics does not straightforwardly disclose its inner furniture. He argues that scientific theories function primarily as systematic representations of observable laws, and rejects the view that successful theory automatically licenses strong metaphysical conclusions about unobserva

philosophers.label: Alain de Botton
key_works: How Proust Can Change Your Life
philosophers.profile: Alain de Botton’s philosophy is best understood as a practical, therapeutic humanism that refunctions philosophical reflection for the management of ordinary suffering. Ontologically, he does not construct a systematic metaphysics; he presupposes a broadly secular, naturalistic world in which human beings are psychologically fragile, socially dependent, and symbolically formed by institutions, narratives, and aesthetic environments. His central concern is less being as such than the conditions under which finite agents can live well amid anxiety, status competition, love, work, and mortality.
philosophers.stance: De Botton holds that reality is not best understood through speculative metaphysics but through the lived structures of human vulnerability, attachment, aspiration, and social comparison. He argues that the most salient features of reality for philosophy are psychological and institutional rather than ultimate substances or first principles. About knowledge, he holds that human beings know poorly

philosophers.label: Cornelius Castoriadis
key_works: The Institution of Society and Religion
philosophers.profile: Cornelius Castoriadis develops an ontology of radical creation centered on the imaginary institution of society. Against both substantialist metaphysics and deterministic materialism, he posits being as stratified and irreducibly creative: not exhausted by ensidic (ensemblistic-identitary) determination, but traversed by a magma from which new forms emerge that cannot be deduced from prior states. Social-historical reality is therefore neither epiphenomenal nor mechanically necessitated; it is instituted through collective imaginaries that generate significations, norms, and forms of life. His
philosophers.stance: Castoriadis holds that reality is not a fully determinate order governed by exhaustive laws, but includes genuinely creative emergence. He argues that social reality is instituted through shared meanings that are neither mere subjective projections nor simple reflections of material conditions. He therefore rejects the view that history can be explained by strict causal necessity or by a closed ra

philosophers.label: Vinayak Damodar Savarkar
key_works: The Indian War of Independence of 1857
Six Glorious Epochs of Indian History
philosophers.profile: Vinayak Damodar Savarkar articulates a militantly this-worldly and collectivist political philosophy centered on the idea of Hindutva as a civilizational principle rather than a narrowly confessional creed. Ontologically, he treats the nation not as a mere juridical aggregate but as an historically sedimented cultural organism constituted by common territory, blood, memory, and sacred geography. The real unit of political being is thus the people understood as an inherited and self-conscious collective. Epistemologically, he privileges historical consciousness, political realism, and strategic
philosophers.stance: Savarkar holds that reality in the politically relevant sense is not exhausted by isolated individuals; enduring collectivities such as nations are historically real and normatively basic. He argues that social and political life is structured by power, memory, territorial attachment, and collective identity, and rejects the view that universal moral sentiment alone can ground political order. philosophers.label: Brihaspati
philosophers.profile: Brihaspati is traditionally associated with the materialist current later called Lokayata or Carvaka, though the attribution is textually unstable and survives largely through hostile reconstruction. In that lineage, ontology is resolutely this-worldly: only the bhutas or material elements are real, and consciousness is an emergent property of embodied aggregation rather than an independent atman. The self is thus identified with the living body endowed with sentience, not with a transmigrating substance. Causation is treated in broadly naturalistic terms, without appeal to adrishta, karma, or
philosophers.stance: This thinker is taken to hold that reality is fundamentally material and that only perceptible entities deserve full ontological commitment. He argues that consciousness depends on bodily organization and rejects the view that there is an immortal self separable from the body. He also rejects unseen causal forces invoked to explain fate, ritual efficacy, or postmortem survival. About the grounds o

philosophers.label: Nicolaus of Damascus
philosophers.profile: Nicolaus of Damascus is best understood as an eclectic moral and political thinker whose philosophy mediates between Peripatetic conceptuality and the practical concerns of statesmanship. His surviving philosophical orientation suggests a commitment to a teleological and hierarchically ordered cosmos in which natural beings possess determinate functions and human flourishing depends upon the right cultivation of rational capacities. Ontologically, he appears to assume a realist framework: the world is not a projection of mind but an intelligible order structured by natures, causes, and ends. E
philosophers.stance: Nicolaus of Damascus holds that reality is an ordered and intelligible whole composed of beings with determinate natures and ends. He argues that human beings are not ethically self-sufficient apart from this order; flourishing depends on aligning reason, character, and action with the proper function of human life. He rejects the view that knowledge is either purely deductive or merely sensory: t

philosophers.label: Lucien Lévy-Bruhl
key_works: La morale et la science des moeurs
Les fonctions mentales dans les sociétés inférieures
L'expérience mystique et les symboles chez les primitifs
philosophers.profile: Lucien Lévy-Bruhl develops a philosophical anthropology centered on the plurality of mental orientations and the irreducibility of collective representations. Against the assumption that all cognition is fundamentally governed by the same logical canons, he argues that many social worlds are structured by a mode of thought he initially characterizes as “prelogical,” not in the sense of irrational defect, but as governed by participation rather than by strict adherence to the principles of identity, non-contradiction, and causal individuation. Ontologically, reality is not given as a set of dis
philosophers.stance: Lévy-Bruhl holds that reality, as lived and interpreted by human groups, is not always apprehended as a collection of separate objects governed solely by impersonal causal relations; it may also be constituted through relations of participation, symbolic linkage, and sacred efficacy. He argues that the grounds of knowledge are not reducible to individual sense-data or pure inference, but are media

philosophers.label: Saadia Gaon
philosophers.profile: Saadia Gaon articulates a systematic Jewish kalam in which created being, divine unity, and revealed law are rendered philosophically demonstrable. His ontology is governed by the absolute distinction between Creator and creature: God alone is uncaused, simple, living, powerful, and wise, while the world is temporally originated ex nihilo and therefore radically contingent. Against eternalist cosmologies, he defends creation through arguments from the finitude and composite character of bodies, time, and motion. Divine attributes are treated so as to preserve tawhid-like simplicity without eva
philosophers.stance: Saadia Gaon holds that reality is fundamentally dual in the sense of an absolute distinction between an uncreated, simple God and a created, contingent world. He argues that the cosmos had a beginning and rejects the view that matter or motion is eternal. He holds that universes of bodies, time, and change require a transcendent cause, and that divine unity excludes any literal multiplicity in God

philosophers.label: Martianus Capella
philosophers.profile: Martianus Capella presents a cosmological and pedagogical vision in which the order of reality is mirrored by the order of the liberal arts. In his allegorical synthesis, ontology is fundamentally hierarchical and intelligible: the cosmos is structured, ensouled, and governed by a rational principle that links celestial order, divine administration, and human cultivation. Being is not treated through systematic metaphysics in the manner of a technical σχολή, yet his work presupposes a graded universe in which lower domains participate in higher intelligibility. Epistemologically, knowledge is
philosophers.stance: Martianus Capella holds that reality is an ordered and intelligible whole, structured in levels and bound together by rational, mathematical, and symbolic relations. He argues that the world is not a mere aggregate of facts but a cosmos whose inner articulation can be progressively disclosed through disciplined study. On the grounds of knowledge, he holds that understanding arises through intellec

philosophers.label: Francisco de Vitoria
philosophers.profile: Francisco de Vitoria articulates a Thomist account of being, law, and political community in which created reality is intelligible through participation in an ordered natural and divine teleology. His ontology is realist: substances, natures, ends, and relations are not mere conceptual projections but features of a created order grounded in divine wisdom. In epistemology, he works within moderate scholastic realism, holding that the intellect attains universal truths by abstraction from sense experience, while practical reason discerns normative principles through synderesis and the natural la
philosophers.stance: Vitoria holds that reality is a mind-independent, purposive order in which human beings, communities, and norms have determinate natures. He argues that political and juridical relations are not reducible to convention alone, because there are objective principles of right rooted in rational nature and the common good. He rejects the view that sovereignty is unlimited or that religious difference

philosophers.label: Luitzen Egbertus Jan Brouwer
philosophers.profile: Brouwer’s philosophy is centered on intuitionism, according to which mathematics is not a body of truths about an independently given abstract domain but a free construction of the thinking subject grounded in the primordial intuition of inner time. Ontologically, he denies that mathematical objects subsist as completed entities apart from acts of construction; the continuum is not an actually completed set of points but a medium generated through lawful mental activity. Epistemologically, mathematical knowledge arises from evident construction, not from linguistic derivation or formal proof a
philosophers.stance: Brouwer holds that reality, at least in the mathematical domain, is not fundamentally a ready-made order of independently existing abstract objects; it is constituted through constructive mental activity grounded in temporal intuition. He argues that existence claims are legitimate only when one can in principle exhibit a construction, and rejects the view that truth outruns possible demonstration

philosophers.label: Charles Taylor
philosophers.profile: Charles Taylor develops a philosophical anthropology centered on the self as a self-interpreting animal whose agency is constituted within webs of interlocution, language, and historically sedimented meanings. Against atomism and naturalist reduction, he argues that human action is irreducibly shaped by strong evaluation: agents do not merely rank desires instrumentally but discriminate qualitatively among goods according to their worth. His ontology is thus anti-reductionist and pluralistic, affirming that persons inhabit moral space structured by constitutive goods, social imaginaries, and b
philosophers.stance: Taylor holds that reality includes not only physical processes but irreducible domains of meaning, value, and social practice. He argues that human beings are constituted by interpretive frameworks and that social reality is partly made by shared understandings rather than being fully describable in purely external terms. He rejects the view that knowledge rests on a detached, self-sufficient subj

philosophers.label: Shulamith Firestone
key_works: The Dialectic of Sex: The Case for Feminist Revolution
philosophers.profile: Shulamith Firestone develops a radical feminist materialism in which sexual class, rather than merely economic class, is treated as a fundamental structuring principle of social reality. Her ontology is historical and constructivist: the family, gender, romance, and motherhood are not natural givens but institutionalized forms produced through biological asymmetry and reproduced by ideology. She treats the sex distinction as the basis of a political antagonism whose deepest mechanism lies in women’s reproductive vulnerability and children’s dependence. Epistemically, Firestone privileges consc
philosophers.stance: Firestone holds that social reality is structured by domination at the level of reproduction, kinship, and sexuality, and that what is often taken as natural sex difference is in fact politically organized into a hierarchy. She argues that reality is neither reducible to ideas nor exhausted by economics; rather, material embodiment and institutional arrangements jointly generate sexual class. On k

philosophers.label: Donna Haraway
philosophers.profile: Donna Haraway develops a material-semiotic ontology in which beings do not preexist their relations but emerge through situated practices of articulation, technoscientific mediation, and multispecies entanglement. She rejects both reductive naturalism and disembodied social constructivism, arguing instead that nature, culture, organism, and machine are co-constituted within historically specific apparatuses of production, representation, and control. Her epistemology is centered on situated knowledges: objectivity is not the transcendence of perspective but accountable, partial, embodied visio
philosophers.stance: Haraway holds that reality is neither a fixed inventory of self-subsistent substances nor a mere projection of discourse; it is a field of relationally constituted beings produced through material-semiotic practices. She argues that entities become what they are within networks of interaction, instrumentation, narration, labor, and ecological interdependence. About the grounds of knowledge, she re

philosophers.label: Flora Tristan
key_works: Nécessité de faire un bon accueil aux femmes étrangères
philosophers.profile: Flora Tristan develops a social philosophy in which the atomized individual of bourgeois civil society is exposed as an abstraction sustained by juridical, economic, and sexual domination. Her ontology is implicitly relational and social: human beings are constituted through labor, dependence, and reciprocal recognition, yet existing institutions deform these relations into hierarchy, pauperization, and the subordination of women. She treats the social body as an intelligible totality whose pathologies cannot be grasped by isolated moral judgment alone. Epistemically, she privileges inquiry gr
philosophers.stance: Tristan holds that reality is not best understood as a collection of self-sufficient individuals but as a structured social order shaped by labor, power, dependency, and recognition. She argues that social facts are real and causally efficacious, and that domination is reproduced across economic and familial institutions. On knowledge, she holds that understanding injustice requires attention to c

philosophers.label: Gene Sharp
philosophers.profile: Gene Sharp develops a theory of power in which political rule is not a self-subsisting substance but a relational effect generated by obedience, cooperation, and the routine performance of social roles. His ontology of the political is therefore anti-substantialist: institutions endure only insofar as subjects, organizations, and administrative strata reproduce them through compliance. From this follows his central thesis that power is pluralized across society and that domination is vulnerable at its sources. Epistemically, Sharp proceeds through a strategic and quasi-empirical analysis of co
philosophers.stance: Sharp holds that political reality is constituted less by sheer force than by structured patterns of obedience, institutional cooperation, and belief in legitimacy. He argues that domination persists only while its supporting networks continue to function, and rejects the view that state power is ultimately irresistible or metaphysically self-grounding. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds tha

philosophers.label: Michael Polanyi
philosophers.profile: Michael Polanyi develops a post-positivist epistemology centered on tacit knowing, personal participation, and the fiduciary structure of inquiry. Against objectivist accounts that construe knowledge as wholly explicit, impersonal, and method-governed, he argues that all acts of knowing involve subsidiary awareness integrated into a focal apprehension: we know more than we can tell. This from-to structure of consciousness underwrites skill, perception, scientific discovery, and linguistic understanding. Ontologically, Polanyi defends a stratified realism in which higher levels of order emerge
philosophers.stance: Polanyi holds that reality is layered and mind-independent, but not fully capturable by reductionist explanation or explicit formalization. He argues that higher-order realities such as life, consciousness, and meaning are genuine features of the world, governed by organizing principles that cannot be eliminated in favor of lower-level descriptions. philosophers.label: Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi
key_works: On the Doctrine of Spinoza in Letters to Moses Mendelssohn
David Hume on Faith, or Idealism and Realism
Concerning the Attempt of the Criticism to Bring Reason to Understanding
Of Divine Things and Their Revelation
philosophers.profile: Friedrich Heinrich Jacobi develops a critique of discursive reason that turns on the claim that demonstrative philosophy, when consistently pursued, terminates in fatalism, mechanism, or nihilism. Against the pretension that the unconditioned can be deduced from conditioned relations, he maintains that all proof proceeds mediately and therefore never reaches the absolutely first. Immediate certainty, which he calls Glaube, is not arbitrary opinion but the originary mode in which reality, freedom, God, and the existence of other beings are given. Ontologically, he resists both reductive natural
philosophers.stance: Jacobi holds that reality is fundamentally not exhausted by conceptual mediation or causal explanation. He argues that the real includes irreducible individuality, freedom, and a transcendent divine source, and rejects the view that being can be fully reconstructed from impersonal necessity. About the grounds of knowledge, he holds that all inferential cognition depends on a prior non-inferential

philosophers.label: Synesius of Cyrene
philosophers.profile: Synesius of Cyrene develops a distinctly Neoplatonic metaphysics inflected by Christian theological commitments. Reality is hierarchically ordered: the sensible cosmos is a derivative manifestation of higher intelligible principles, and the soul occupies a mediating station between corporeal multiplicity and noetic unity. His ontology affirms the priority of immaterial being over material extension, while treating the cosmos as a symbolically charged and providentially ordered whole rather than a brute aggregate of bodies. The human soul is not exhausted by embodied life; it bears an intrinsic
philosophers.stance: Synesius holds that reality is fundamentally stratified, with intelligible and immaterial being more basic than the sensory world. He argues that the visible cosmos is real but ontologically dependent, expressive of higher principles rather than self-sufficient. He rejects the view that knowledge arises from sensation alone, holding instead that the highest cognition depends on intellectual ascent

philosophers.label: Antonio Labriola
key_works: In Memory of the Communist Manifesto
Essays on the Materialistic Conception of History
Discorrendo di socialismo e di filosofia
philosophers.profile: Antonio Labriola develops a non-reductive historical materialism centered on praxis, social mediation, and the immanent intelligibility of historical life. He rejects both speculative metaphysics and vulgar materialism: reality is not a static substrate governed by abstract laws, but the historically determinate ensemble of social relations reproduced through labor, conflict, and institutional forms. His ontology is thus relational and processual; social being is constituted through practical activity, and consciousness is neither an autonomous origin nor a mere epiphenomenon, but a mediated m
philosophers.stance: Labriola holds that reality is fundamentally historical, social, and processual rather than composed of fixed essences or reducible to isolated individuals. He argues that the most important structures of human reality are relations of production, labor, institutions, and forms of collective practice. He rejects the view that ideas alone drive history, but also rejects crude reductionism in which

philosophers.label: Marsilius of Padua
concepts: separation of spiritual and temporal power
philosophers.profile: Marsilius of Padua develops a rigorously naturalistic and civic conception of political order in which the civitas is a self-sufficient community ordered to sufficient life through human legislation rather than sacerdotal domination. His ontology of the political is broadly Aristotelian: the political community is a composite whole whose parts possess differentiated functions, yet its unity is not mystical but juridical and institutional. The efficient cause of law is the universitas civium or its valentior pars, whose authoritative will gives positive law its binding force; the ruler is princ
philosophers.stance: Marsilius holds that political reality is a humanly organized order grounded in the natural sociability of persons and the institutional structure of a community, not in a sacral hierarchy with intrinsic coercive authority. He argues that law derives its authority from the collective body of citizens, or the weightier part thereof, and rejects the view that religious office by itself confers juris

philosophers.label: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
key_works: Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience
Creativity: Flow and the Psychology of Discovery and Invention
philosophers.profile: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi develops a eudaimonistic psychology centered on the structure of optimal experience. His central thesis is that consciousness is not merely a passive receptacle of stimuli but an ordered field of attention whose quality determines the worth of a life. Ontologically, he treats the human world as a transactional nexus of person, symbolic environment, and culturally sedimented practices; reality as lived is mediated by attentional organization and by the affordances of domains in which action becomes intelligible. Epistemically, he privileges phenomenological report, longi
philosophers.stance: Csikszentmihalyi holds that reality, as it matters for human flourishing, is not exhausted by external events but is significantly constituted by the organization of consciousness and the relation between agent and environment. He argues that the quality of life depends less on possession or pleasure than on the structure of experience, especially experiences of absorbed, skillful activity. About

philosophers.label: Max Wertheimer
key_works: Experimental Studies on the Perception of Movement
Laws of Organization in Perceptual Forms
philosophers.profile: Max Wertheimer develops a non-atomistic account of mind and world in which primary intelligibility belongs to structured wholes rather than to aggregates of independent elements. Against elementarist psychologies that construe perception as a summation of sensations, he argues that phenomenal organization exhibits intrinsic form-laws: the given is articulated according to relations of proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, and figure-ground segregation. His ontology is thus relational and structural: what is real for cognition is not a mere heap of parts but a dynamically ordered Gestalt
philosophers.stance: Wertheimer holds that reality, at least as it is available to cognition, is fundamentally organized in structured patterns rather than built up from self-sufficient atomic units. He argues that wholes are not reducible to the sum of parts, because the parts themselves are what they are only within an ordered relational field. philosophers.label: Strato of Lampsacus
philosophers.profile: Strato of Lampsacus develops a rigorously naturalistic Peripatetic philosophy in which physis is treated as an immanent principle of motion, generation, and organization rather than as the execution of transcendent or anthropomorphic purposiveness. He is associated with a marked attenuation of formal and final causation in favor of efficient and material explanation, so that natural processes are understood through the intrinsic powers and tendencies of bodies themselves. His ontology is thus strongly physicalist: reality consists in corporeal beings and the dynamic structures of nature, with
philosophers.stance: Strato holds that reality is fundamentally a self-organizing natural order composed of bodies and their powers. He argues that change, life, and cosmic order can be explained by immanent causal processes, and rejects the view that nature must be understood primarily through externally imposed purposes or divine design. On the nature of reality, he therefore occupies a robust naturalist and realist

philosophers.label: Samuel von Pufendorf
key_works: De habitu religionis christianae ad vitam civilem
philosophers.profile: Samuel von Pufendorf develops a systematic doctrine of natural law grounded in the condition of human sociability (socialitas) and the normative structure of moral entities (entia moralia). He distinguishes sharply between the physical order of nature and the juridical-moral order instituted through reason, imputation, obligation, office, status, and personhood. Ontologically, he does not treat moral norms as intrinsic teleological forms in things, but as binding relations superadded to natural beings through divine ordination and rational apprehension. Human beings, considered in their natura
philosophers.stance: Pufendorf holds that reality contains both natural beings and a distinct layer of normative statuses, relations, and obligations that are not reducible to brute physical facts. He argues that political and legal order is artificial rather than organically given: states, offices, rights, and sovereignty are constituted through acts, conventions, and imputed norms.
